slides.html:
What is the Turning Effect of Forces?

The turning effect of a force is also called the moment of force or torque. It depends on two factors:

  • The magnitude of the force applied
  • The perpendicular distance from the pivot point to the line of action of the force

This distance is called the moment arm or lever arm.

Formula: Moment = Force × Perpendicular distance

Or mathematically: \(M = F \times d\)

Where:
\(\ M\) = moment (Nm)
\(\ F\) = force (N)
\(\ d\) = perpendicular distance (m)

quiz.prompt: A force of 20 N is applied at a distance of 0.5 m from a pivot. What is the moment of force?

slides.html:
Principle of Moments

For a body to be in equilibrium (not rotating), the sum of clockwise moments must equal the sum of anticlockwise moments.

Clockwise Moment = Anticlockwise Moment

This principle is used in:

  • Seesaws
  • Balance scales
  • Lever systems
  • Bridge construction

Example: A 50 N weight is placed 2 m from a pivot. To balance it, you need a 25 N weight placed 4 m from the pivot on the other side.

Why? Because \(50 \times 2 = 25 \times 4 = 100\) Nm

quiz.prompt: A seesaw has a child weighing 300 N sitting 1.5 m from the pivot. Where should a child weighing 200 N sit to balance the seesaw?

slides.html:
Types of Levers

There are three types of levers, classified by the position of the effort, load, and fulcrum:

1. First Class Lever

Fulcrum is between effort and load

Examples: Seesaw, crowbar, scissors

Mechanical Advantage: Can be greater or less than 1

2. Second Class Lever

Load is between effort and fulcrum

Examples: Wheelbarrow, nutcracker, bottle opener

Mechanical Advantage: Always greater than 1

3. Third Class Lever

Effort is between load and fulcrum

Examples: Fishing rod, human arm, tweezers

Mechanical Advantage: Always less than 1

quiz.prompt: Which of these is a second class lever?

slides.html:
Centre of Gravity

The centre of gravity is the point where the entire weight of the body acts. For stability:

  • The lower the centre of gravity, the more stable the object
  • The centre of gravity should be above the base of support

Examples of stability:

  • A wide-based object is more stable than a narrow-based one
  • A car rolls over when its centre of gravity falls outside its base

Calculating Centre of Gravity:

For multiple point masses: \(x_{cg} = \frac{m_1x_1 + m_2x_2 + ...}{m_1 + m_2 + ...}\)

Where \(x_{cg}\) is the position of the centre of gravity.

quiz.prompt: Why is a cone more stable than a cylinder of the same height and mass?
choices: It has a lower centre of gravity
It has a higher centre of gravity
